questions.question: Let G be a connected planar graph with 10 vertices. If the number of edges on each face is three, then the number of edges in G is _______.
questions.to_find: Number of edges E in the connected planar graph G

understand.plain: When a connected graph is drawn in the plane without edge crossings, it splits the plane into regions called faces (one of them is the unbounded outer region). Two facts pin everything down: Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 links vertices, edges and faces, and a face-counting argument says that adding the number of edges around every face counts each edge exactly twice. Here every face is bounded by 3 edges, so we get a second equation and solve the two together.

keywords.explain: A region of the plane enclosed by edges of a planar drawing; the single unbounded region outside the graph also counts as one face.
keywords.example: A triangle drawn in the plane has 2 faces: the inside region and the outside region.

keywords.explain: For any connected planar graph, V - E + F = 2, where V, E, F are the counts of vertices, edges and faces.
keywords.example: A square (4 vertices, 4 edges): 4 - 4 + F = 2 gives F = 2, matching inside + outside.

keywords.explain: If you add up the number of edges bordering each face, every edge is counted twice because it lies between two faces.
keywords.example: If 6 faces each border 3 edges, the total 6*3 = 18 equals 2E, so E = 9.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='458' height='238' class='bg'/><circle cx='120' cy='40' r='6' class='ink'/><circle cx='60' cy='150' r='6' class='ink'/><circle cx='180' cy='150' r='6' class='ink'/><circle cx='120' cy='100' r='6' class='ink'/><line x1='120' y1='40' x2='60' y2='150' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='120' y1='40' x2='180' y2='150' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='180' y2='150' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='120' y1='40' x2='120' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='60' y1='150' x2='120' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='180' y1='150' x2='120' y2='100' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='118' y='95' class='sub mut'>tri-faces</text><text x='250' y='70' class='lbl ink'>Every face = 3 edges</text><text x='250' y='110' class='lbl ink'>3F = 2E</text><text x='250' y='150' class='lbl ink'>V - E + F = 2</text><text x='250' y='190' class='big ok'>E = 24</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A triangulated planar drawing where each region is a triangle, illustrating that 3F = 2E combined with Euler's formula gives E = 24.

given.aim: Find the number of edges in a connected planar graph on 10 vertices in which every face is bounded by exactly 3 edges.

terms.connects: Appears in Euler's formula V - E + F = 2

terms.meaning: Number of edges in G (the unknown)
terms.example: We will solve for E and obtain 24
terms.connects: Linked to faces by 3F = 2E and to V, F by Euler's formula

terms.meaning: Number of faces (regions) in the planar drawing, including the outer face
terms.example: Determined from Euler's formula as F = E - 8

terms.meaning: Number of edges on the boundary of a face; here it is 3 for every face
terms.connects: Summed over all faces it equals 2E (each edge counted twice)

given.plan: Use the face-counting relation 3F = 2E to express F in terms of E, then substitute into Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 with V = 10 and solve the resulting linear equation for E.

steps.title: Count edges via face degrees
steps.apply: Every face is bounded by 3 edges, so the sum of all face degrees is 3F. Since each edge lies on exactly two faces, this sum equals 2E. Hence 3F = 2E, giving F = 2E/3.
steps.note: This double-counting works because in a connected planar graph every edge borders exactly two faces.

steps.title: Apply Euler's formula
steps.apply: Substitute V = 10 and F = 2E/3: 10 - E + 2E/3 = 2. Then 10 - E/3 = 2, so E/3 = 8, giving E = 24. (Check: F = 2*24/3 = 16, and 10 - 24 + 16 = 2.)
steps.note: The fractional coefficient -E + 2E/3 = -E/3 is what makes the solve clean; always verify with Euler's identity at the end.

questions.formula_note: The pair 3F = 2E (every face a triangle) and Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 is the key combination; eliminating F turns the problem into a single linear equation E/3 = 8.

questions.question: In an undirected connected planar graph G, there are eight vertices and five faces. The number of edges in G is _______.
questions.to_find: Number of edges E in the connected planar graph G

understand.plain: This is a direct application of Euler's formula for connected planar graphs: V - E + F = 2. The number of vertices and faces are both handed to you, so the only unknown is the number of edges. Plug in V = 8 and F = 5 and rearrange to read off E.

keywords.explain: For any connected planar graph drawn without crossings, V - E + F = 2 always holds.
keywords.example: A path with no cycle still works if it is just one face: a triangle has 3 - 3 + 2 = 2.

keywords.explain: The number of regions the planar drawing divides the plane into, counting the unbounded outer region.
keywords.example: Here F = 5 means four bounded regions plus one outer region.

keywords.explain: A graph drawable in the plane with no edge crossings and in one piece; only connected graphs satisfy V - E + F = 2 exactly.
keywords.example: G with 8 vertices all reachable from one another, drawn flat with 5 faces.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 440 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='1' y='1' width='438' height='198' class='bg'/><text x='30' y='55' class='lbl ink'>V = 8</text><text x='30' y='95' class='lbl ink'>F = 5</text><text x='30' y='135' class='lbl mut'>V - E + F = 2</text><line x1='200' y1='30' x2='200' y2='170' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='250' y='70' class='lbl ink'>8 - E + 5 = 2</text><text x='250' y='110' class='lbl ink'>13 - E = 2</text><text x='250' y='155' class='big ok'>E = 11</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A worked panel substituting V = 8 and F = 5 into Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 to obtain E = 11.

given.aim: Find the number of edges in a connected planar graph that has 8 vertices and 5 faces.

terms.connects: First term of Euler's formula V - E + F = 2

terms.meaning: Number of faces, including the outer unbounded face
terms.connects: Third term of Euler's formula V - E + F = 2
Middle term of Euler's formula; isolated to find the answer

given.plan: Substitute the given V = 8 and F = 5 directly into Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 and solve the single linear equation for E.

steps.title: Write Euler's formula
steps.apply: G is connected and planar, so Euler's formula applies directly with V = 8 and F = 5.
steps.note: Connectivity is essential; the +2 on the right is exact only for a single connected component.

steps.title: Substitute and solve for E
steps.apply: From 8 - E + 5 = 2 we get 13 - E = 2, hence E = 11.
steps.note: Pure arithmetic rearrangement of the linear equation.

questions.formula_note: Euler's formula V - E + F = 2 for connected planar graphs is the only tool needed; with V and F given, E falls out immediately as 11.

formulas.name: Euler's Formula for Connected Planar Graphs
formulas.whenToUse: Use for any connected graph drawn in the plane without edge crossings to relate the number of vertices V, edges E and faces F (the outer region counts as a face).

terms.means: number of faces, including the unbounded outer face

formulas.trap: Holds only for connected planar graphs; for a planar graph with c connected components the relation becomes V - E + F = 1 + c, and remember to count the outer region as a face.

formulas.name: Sum of Face Degrees Equals Twice the Edges
formulas.plain: sum over all faces of (edges bounding that face) = 2E
formulas.whenToUse: Use in a planar embedding to relate faces to edges: each edge borders exactly two faces, so adding up the boundary-edge count of every face double-counts the edges.

terms.means: number of edges on the boundary of face f
number of faces; if every face has degree k then kF = 2E

formulas.trap: A bridge (cut edge) is counted twice for the same face, and the outer face must be included; if all faces have the same degree k then kF = 2E, e.g. triangular faces give 3F = 2E.
formulas.reference: Handshaking lemma applied to the dual / faces of a planar graph
